text: Control over the photochemical excited state behavior of first-row transition metal complexes has long been an area of challenge and interest in inorganic photochemistry. While many $d^6$ precious metal complexes such as Ru(II), Os(II) and Ir(III) derivatives have been heavily utilized in recent years in applications such as photocatalysis and solar energy conversion, their earth abundant 3$d$ counterparts are more often than not limited by their ultrafast non-radiative quenching through short-lived metal centred states. Here we report that by employing chelate ligands with high ligand field energies and a high degree of structural constraint about the metal centre, we can create Cr(0) derivatives of tris(diisocyanide) character which exhibit metal to ligand charge transfer excited states with lifetimes of up to 47 ns and with photoluminescence quantum yields of over 1% in solution at room temperature. These values are comparable to the best reported Os(II) derivatives. Utilizing the high oxidising power of the excited state (Eox∗ ≈ -2.7 V vs Fc+/0) derived from these Cr(0) luminophores, we are able to act as catalysts in demanding photoredox processes which include the reduction of aryl halides and overall redox neutral coupling reactions between different organic fragments achieved under red-light illumination. In summary, we show that it is possible to create 3$d^6$ first row transition metal photochemical catalysts that are competitive to their precious metal analogues through judicious molecular design.
